headline: The response changes before the story changes.
dek: BrainDiff reads the two videos as timelines, finds where attention and social meaning bend, and gives you the exact moments worth recutting.

a.label: A
a.name: control_cut.mp4
a.summary: A quieter opening, slower product reveal, and a measured closing frame.

scenes.title: Cold open
scenes.beat: Hands enter frame before context lands.
scenes.visual: Soft beige desk, slow camera drift

scenes.title: Proof
scenes.beat: The product appears after the setup has settled.

scenes.title: Human read
scenes.beat: A face reaction carries the middle section.

b.label: B
b.name: active_cut.mp4
b.summary: A faster first frame, earlier face cue, and a sharper end card.

scenes.title: Instant hook
scenes.beat: Motion and face appear in the first second.

scenes.title: Tension
scenes.beat: The object and person alternate quickly.
scenes.visual: Jump cuts, red detail in frame

scenes.title: Payoff
scenes.beat: The strongest social cue sits before the product claim.

scenes.title: Hard close
scenes.beat: The call-to-action lands with motion still active.

moments.title: First second earns the look
moments.detail: The face plus camera push gives B an early attention lift before the claim appears.
moments.move: Keep the human cue in the opening beat.

moments.title: Payoff carries the person, not the product
moments.detail: The strongest social response happens when the viewer reads the reaction shot.
moments.move: Place the product claim after this beat, not before it.

moments.title: A closes cleaner
moments.detail: The calmer end card gives A a small memory advantage even though it is less energetic.
moments.move: Borrow A's quieter last two seconds for B's end card.

recommendations: Lead with B's first-frame motion and face cue.
Keep B's mid-video social payoff, then slow down the final card.
Do not optimize only for total heat. The best cut is B's opening with A's close.
